sections.biography-source-undefined:
Cao Yu was styled Pengzu. He was humble by nature. Cao Yu held several feudal titles over his lifetime, but the one he held for the majority of his life was the Princedom of Yan based in Ye.

When Cao Rui was young, Cao Yu helped look after him and they grew close, and when Cao Rui became emperor, he showered Cao Yu with great favor and gifts. Cao Yu is recorded to have often visited the capital.

In 239, Cao Rui fell gravely ill, and his heir, Cao Fang, was very young. Cao Yu was quickly made Grand General to assist Cao Fang, but only after four days, he “firmly” signaled his intent to resign. Cao Rui had also been convinced that the appointment of Cao Yu was not wise and removed Cao Yu from his position. Cao Yu, along with Cao Zhao, Qin Lang, and Xiahou Xian, were prevented from being regents to Cao Fang; they were replaced by Cao Shuang and Sima Yi in that capacity. Sima Yi was not in the capital region at the time, so Cao Yu suggested to Cao Rui that Sima Yi should instead go to the Chang’an region to oversee matters there. Although Cao Rui issued an edict to Sima Yi according to what Cao Yu suggested, he changed his mind and issued a second edict having Sima Yi be a regent. Cao Yu returned to Ye shortly after Cao Rui died.

He would still be greatly favored by the rulers of the Wei dynasty, and he also had favor with the Sima clan. His treatment was so great that only Sima Zhao surpassed him in privilege, indicating that Cao Yu was the most favored of the Wei princes.(JS 2) One of his sons, Cao Huan, was chosen to succeed Cao Mao as emperor by the Sima clan. He retained the title of Duke of Yan during the Jin dynasty.

full_text:
Cao Yu (Pengzu) 曹宇 (彭祖)
ARomance of the Three KingdomsEncyclopedia officer profile.
Cao Yu (曹宇), styledPengzu (彭祖), was born in Pei. Refused to be Regent to Cao Fang due to his modest nature. Cao Yu was affiliated with the Wei Kingdom and the Jin Dynasty. Cao Yu lived during the Later Han Dynasty and Three Kingdoms periods of Chinese history.

BiographySource Undefined
Cao Yu was styled Pengzu. He was humble by nature. Cao Yu held several feudal titles over his lifetime, but the one he held for the majority of his life was the Princedom of Yan based in Ye.
When Cao Rui was young, Cao Yu helped look after him and they grew close, and when Cao Rui became emperor, he showered Cao Yu with great favor and gifts. Cao Yu is recorded to have often visited the capital.
In 239, Cao Rui fell gravely ill, and his heir, Cao Fang, was very young. Cao Yu was quickly made Grand General to assist Cao Fang, but only after four days, he “firmly” signaled his intent to resign. Cao Rui had also been convinced that the appointment of Cao Yu was not wise and removed Cao Yu from his position. Cao Yu, along with Cao Zhao, Qin Lang, and Xiahou Xian, were prevented from being regents to Cao Fang; they were replaced by Cao Shuang and Sima Yi in that capacity. Sima Yi was not in the capital region at the time, so Cao Yu suggested to Cao Rui that Sima Yi should instead go to the Chang’an region to oversee matters there. Although Cao Rui issued an edict to Sima Yi according to what Cao Yu suggested, he changed his mind and issued a second edict having Sima Yi be a regent. His treatment was so great that only Sima Zhao surpassed him in privilege, indicating that Cao Yu was the most favored of the Wei princes.(JS 2) One of his sons, Cao Huan, was chosen to succeed Cao Mao as emperor by the Sima clan. He retained the title of Duke of Yan during the Jin dynasty.
